did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of the AI-based smartphone app SkinVision® (which uses machine learning algorithms for risk stratification of pigmented skin lesions) and compared its performance against 2D and 3D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) devices (FotoFinder ATBM® and Vectra® WB360) in the context of melanoma detection and risk assessment.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The study is a prospective, single-center, comparative observational cohort study that investigates the diagnostic accuracy of the SkinVision® smartphone app in melanoma detection. It involves original data collection from 114 patients and 7 dermatologists, assessment of lesions using multiple devices and clinical standards, and statistical analysis of sensitivity, specificity, and patient/dermatologist satisfaction, which constitutes original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: Over-Detection of Melanoma-Suspect Lesions by a CE-Certified Smartphone App: Performance in Comparison to Dermatologists, 2D and 3D Convolutional Neural Networks in a Prospective Data Set of 1204 Pigmented Skin Lesions Involving Patients’ Perception
brief_summary: This prospective study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of the CE-certified smartphone app SkinVision® for melanoma risk stratification compared to dermatologists and 2D/3D Convolutional Neural Network devices. The study assessed 1204 pigmented skin lesions from 114 patients using histological diagnosis or combined expert/AI assessments as reference standards. The app demonstrated low sensitivity (41.3–83.3%) and specificity (60.0–82.9%), with an AUROC of 0.62–0.72, indicating performance below advertised rates. Additionally, the app classified significantly more lesions as high-risk than dermatologists, raising concerns about clinically harmful over-detection and unnecessary excisions. Patient and dermatologist trust in the app was low, with no patients preferring app-only assessment over professional evaluation. The authors conclude that robust prospective evidence is needed before such apps are widely used for public health melanoma screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of risk stratification and detection. Histologically confirmed in 6 lesions out of 61 biopsied. Included 57 patients with previous resected melanoma in situ or primary cutaneous melanoma and 2 with metastatic melanoma.
Benign comparator class. Histologically confirmed in 19 lesions out of 61 biopsied. Used in dichotomous classification (nevus vs. melanoma).
Benign/atypical comparator class. Histologically confirmed in 20 lesions out of 61 biopsied. Included in risk profile criteria for patients.

paper_terms: collisional tumor: seborrheic keratosis and nevus
collisional tumor: actinic keratosis and lentigo solaris
lesion_types_studied.details: Benign or non-melanoma malignant lesions included in the 'otherwise' category for histopathological analysis (16 lesions). Referenced in footnote of Table 3.
The broad category of all 1204 lesions assessed in the study, including melanomas, nevi, and other pigmented entities.

all_task_types.target: Risk category (benign vs. suspicious/melanoma-suspect) and dichotomous diagnosis (nevus vs. melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The SkinVision® app classifies lesions into three different risk scores (benign, intermediate, high-risk) to provide a recommendation to consult a dermatologist. The study also evaluates the app's performance in dichotomous classification of pigmented skin lesions (benign vs. melanoma/suspicious). The 2D FotoFinder ATBM® and 3D Vectra® WB360 devices also provide AI-based risk scores used for classification above study-defined cut-off scores.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Clinical Cohort (University Hospitals of Basel and Zurich)
datasets_used.description: A prospective, single-center (multi-site data collection) observational cohort study involving patients from the Department of Dermatology at the University Hospital of Basel and the University Hospital of Zurich. The dataset consists of pigmented skin lesions assessed by a smartphone app, 2D/3D CNN devices, and dermatologists, with histological diagnosis or combined expert/AI assessment as reference standards.

use.testing: The dataset was used to evaluate the diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, AUROC) of the SkinVision® smartphone app compared to dermatologists and 2D/3D CNN devices. It served as the primary data source for performance assessment against histological and combined reference standards.
use.other: The dataset included 114 patients (55 high-risk for melanoma, 59 melanoma patients). 61 lesions were biopsied for histopathological confirmation. The remaining 1143 lesions were assessed clinically and by AI devices without biopsy, using a combined reference standard of dermatologist evaluation plus AI scores below cut-off.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Comparison of categorical variables; used to compare trustworthiness assessments between patient groups.
Comparison of categorical variables; used in conjunction with Fisher's Exact test for trustworthiness assessments.
To assess the diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, AUC) of the SkinVision® app against combined expert/AI evaluation and against histopathology.
To evaluate variables influencing the trustworthiness of smartphones’ risk assessment in melanoma detection.
To deem statistical comparisons as significant.

hypothesis_testing.test: Wilcoxon rank test, Fisher's Exact test, Pearson’s Chi-squared test
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the distribution of continuous variables (e.g., trust ratings) between groups, or no association between categorical variables (e.g., trustworthiness assessment and patient group or age).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the distribution of continuous variables between groups, or a significant association between categorical variables.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: A p-value of less than 0.05 was deemed as significant. Significant findings included a correlation between age and smartphone trustworthiness (p < 0.004), while no significant differences were found in trust ratings between high-risk and melanoma patient groups (p < 0.9; p < 0.7) or in trustworthiness assessments between patient groups for specific devices (p = 0.3).

reference_standard_combined.description: Combination of dermatologist evaluation plus AI risk assessment scores of FotoFinder ATBM® and Vectra® WB360 below cut-off

Prospective Clinical Cohort (1204 Pigmen.notes: The app classified a significantly higher number of lesions as high-risk than dermatologists. The knowledge of AI-based risk assessment scores did not meaningfully affect dermatologists’ classification, changing decisions for only three lesions. The false-negative rate for all AI-based medical devices was 17%. The true-negative rate (specificity) for melanomas including nevi and other lesions was 60.0% for the smartphone app, 63.6% for Vectra® WB360, and 40.0% for FotoFinder ATBM®.

architecture_used.main_model: Machine-learning algorithm (specific architecture details not reported; referred to as 'AI-based algorithm' or 'deep-learning algorithm' in comparison to other studies)

architecture_details.classifier_head: Risk stratification into three categories: low, medium, and high risk for melanoma

training_details.dataset: Not reported in this study (study evaluated existing app)
training_details.testing: Prospective clinical cohort of 1204 pigmented skin lesions from 114 patients

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinVision® (AI-based smartphone app using machine learning algorithms)
conclusions: The CE-certified smartphone app SkinVision® demonstrated inferior diagnostic accuracy in melanoma detection compared to advertised rates, with sensitivity ranging from 41.3% to 83.3% and specificity from 60.0% to 82.9%.
The app caused clinically harmful over-detection by classifying a significantly higher number of lesions as high-risk than dermatologists, which would lead to a high rate of unnecessary excisions.
Trust in the app was low among both patients and dermatologists, with no patients preferring an assessment by the smartphone app alone over professional evaluation.
The diagnostic accuracy of the app in clinical practice was not as reliable as previously advertised, and satisfaction with smartphone apps for melanoma risk stratification was scarce.

While mHealth apps may increase awareness for melanoma screening in the lay population, healthcare professionals and users should be alerted to the potential harm of over-detection and poor performance.
Robust evidence-based evaluation is needed before including market-approved apps in self-examination for public health benefits.
important_remarks: The study population was a pre-selected cohort of patients at higher risk for melanoma (55 high-risk, 59 melanoma patients), introducing potential selection bias that limits generalizability to the general population.
Photos were taken by medically trained staff in a hospital setting rather than by patients at home, which may overestimate real-world performance due to better image quality and lighting conditions.

Histology was not available for all lesions due to the app's high false-positive rate; using a combined reference standard (dermatologist + AI scores) carries the risk of missing melanomas that both the dermatologist and AI missed.
The study only validated the app on iOS-based smartphones, so diagnostic accuracy differences between iOS and Android devices could not be verified.
The number of histologically confirmed melanomas was very low (n=6), which limits the statistical power and robustness of the sensitivity and specificity estimates for melanoma detection.
The app demonstrated clinically harmful over-detection, classifying 19% of lesions as suspicious compared to only 0.7% by dermatologists, which would lead to a high rate of unnecessary excisions.

The app relies solely on macroscopic images, which is a major limitation compared to dermoscopic images required for accurate diagnosis, potentially explaining the lower AUROC (0.62–0.72) compared to studies using dermoscopy.

The app is CE-certified as a Class 1 device, yet the study found its diagnostic accuracy inferior to advertised rates and to dermatologists, raising concerns about the adequacy of current certification processes for such apps.
